site.name: Gjallr
site.tagline: Rescue dead backed-up websites. Rebuild them properly.
site.description: Gjallr analyses old website folders, SQL dumps, media evidence, URLs, and archive clues, then turns the salvage into a Laravel reconstruction path.

hero.eyebrow: Dead site rescue for Laravel builders
hero.title: Rescue dead backed-up websites. Rebuild them properly.
hero.body: Gjallr finds the useful ore in old website folders, SQL dumps, WordPress installs, custom PHP projects, static exports, and archive snapshots. Then it writes the evidence a builder can hand to an AI with frontend taste.

sections.title: The useful parts survive the fire.
sections.body: Gjallr keeps the material a builder actually needs, without pretending every dead site was WordPress.

items.title: Content and structure
items.body: Posts, pages, custom records, generated HTML, menus, comments, authors, slugs, and dates.

items.title: URLs and redirects
items.body: Legacy paths, query redirects, canonical hints, and enough routing evidence to avoid breaking old links.

items.title: Media evidence
items.body: Upload records, local file checks, recovered archive copies, and clear misses when a file is gone.

items.title: SEO context
items.body: Titles, descriptions, open graph fields, and the old clues that explain why a page mattered.

sections.title: Archive work is evidence, not magic.
sections.body: Internet Archive requests stay opt-in, bounded, and dry-run friendly. Gjallr can check availability, recover missing uploads, and suggest archived URLs. It does not pretend the archive is truth carved into stone.

items.title: Bounded by default
items.body: Limits are explicit, user agents are required for real requests, and dry runs come first.

items.title: Media recovery
items.body: Missing uploads can be recovered into Gjallr-owned storage without overwriting local evidence by accident.

items.title: URL discovery
items.body: Archived HTML paths become suggestions. A builder still decides what belongs in the revived site.

sections.title: Not a dead-site cosplay engine.
sections.body: Gjallr is not here to keep the old machine coughing. It extracts the salvage, names the uncertainty, then gets out of the way.

items.title: No CMS cosplay
items.body: The rescued runtime is read-only. Editing belongs in the rebuild, not in the salvage rig.

items.title: No framework guessing
items.body: WordPress, custom PHP, static exports, and backup folders get inspected before any importer is chosen.

items.title: No finished-site illusion
items.body: The build command writes dossiers. A builder directs the rebuild. A frontend-capable AI can help turn the evidence into the finished Laravel site.

pipeline.title: From archive mess to rebuild dossier.

steps.title: Identify
steps.body: Find likely site roots and SQL dumps across old project folders, hosting backups, duplicated volumes, and dated snapshots.

steps.title: Analyze
steps.body: Inspect filesystem signals and SQL tables before deciding whether this is WordPress, custom PHP, static, mixed, or something worse.

steps.title: Import
steps.body: Map known source types into clean Laravel tables. When no importer exists, write the evidence and mapping plan first.

steps.title: Serve
steps.body: Browse rescued content through normal Laravel routes when a read-only runtime makes sense. No WordPress boot. No plugin roulette.

steps.title: Recover
steps.body: Ask Wayback for missing media and URL evidence when the archive is useful and the limits are clear.

steps.title: Build
steps.body: Write reconstruction dossiers with inventories, paths, media status, redirects, builder notes, and enough context for an AI-assisted frontend rebuild.

skills.title: Teach the agent to stop guessing.
skills.body: The first rescue skill tells an agent how to analyse random old web projects before touching Laravel code. It treats WordPress as one case, not the default answer.

items.title: Pair roots and dumps
items.body: Find likely site roots, nearby SQL dumps, dated backups, duplicated volume copies, and path/name clues that belong together.

items.title: Inspect before importing
items.body: Check entrypoints, config files, table names, prefixes, media folders, admin areas, and generated output before choosing a rescue path.

items.title: Daddy's Birthday rule
items.body: The demo case is not a WordPress proof. It is the reason Gjallr must handle custom PHP and archive mess without making lazy assumptions.

aiHandoff.title: The builder still drives.
aiHandoff.body: Gjallr is meant to hand a website builder clean evidence: source classification, rescued routes, content, media status, SQL clues, archive snapshots, and rebuild notes. The builder can then direct a frontend-skilled AI agent to produce the real site instead of guessing from rubble.

items.title: Give the AI evidence
items.body: Use the dossier, schema notes, JSON page views, recovered media paths, old URLs, and Wayback references as the source material.

items.title: Keep taste with the builder
items.body: The AI can draft layouts, components, copy structure, and responsive details. The builder decides what survives.

items.title: Useful agent brief
items.body: Rebuild this as a clean Laravel frontend. Preserve content intent, routes, SEO context, and media evidence. Do not recreate WordPress.

showcase.title: Make room for the rescue proof.
showcase.body: A real rescue example belongs here: the dead source, the Wayback snapshot, and the rebuilt result. The page is ready for screenshots once the right site turns up.

frames.label: Source
frames.title: Dead source
frames.body: A screenshot of the site as found: missing media, old PHP shell, broken WordPress theme, static export, or whatever state it died in.

frames.label: Wayback
frames.title: Archived reference
frames.body: A Wayback snapshot showing what the site used to look like, useful details, and recovered evidence.

frames.label: Result
frames.title: Rebuilt Laravel site
frames.body: The finished rebuild: clean frontend, preserved content intent, routes kept sane, and the dead source nowhere near production.

boundary.title: Keep the source mess out of the runtime.
boundary.body: WordPress knowledge belongs in ingestion. Archive mechanics belong in Wayback. Runtime models, controllers, routes, and views stay clean. If `wp_*` leaks into the Laravel runtime, something has gone off the rails.

nameStory.title: A short alarm, not a mythology essay.
nameStory.body: Gjallr nods at Gjallarhorn, the horn sounded to wake gods and announce the end of an age. That fits because a dead WordPress site is already the end of an age. Gjallr sounds the alarm, gathers what survived, and turns wreckage into evidence for a proper rebuild.

cta.title: Start with the evidence.
cta.body: The repo has the commands, tests, boundaries, and first rescue skill. Gjallr makes the rubble legible so a builder and a frontend-capable AI can finish the actual site.

footer.text: Built as a static project site. No Laravel runtime. No Jekyll. No ceremony.
